text: The surgeon places three robotic trocars in the left upper quadrant, right lower quadrant, and umbilicus for gallbladder extraction. ICG fluorescence dye and Firefly imaging are used to enhance anatomical delineation during the procedure.

The surgeon uses gentle traction on the gallbladder to identify the cystic duct, visualizing the critical view of safety with the aid of ICG fluorescence dye. The cystic duct and artery are skeletonized to create space for robotic clips. Three clips are placed on the cystic duct after completing the skeletalization.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to ensure an adequate seal before cutting, carefully dissecting the gallbladder from the liver fossa. The scissors aid in minimizing bleeding during the removal. The liver appears fatty, consistent with the patient's history of abnormal liver function.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to take a small biopsy from the liver after successfully removing the gallbladder from the liver bed and achieving excellent hemostasis.

The surgeon uses the robotic system with only one arm to retract the gallbladder, creating tension for optimal positioning. A small liver biopsy is taken from the liver section without cauterization to ensure an adequate tissue sample for diagnosis.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder, using a ten millimeter bag inserted through the eight millimeter trocar site to prevent spillage or contamination of gallstones. The gallbladder is extracted through the umbilical port, and the trocars are closed using a Cutter Thompson and Xeo-Vicro suture to prevent hernias.
